headings.text: What the dial does not show.

paragraphs.text: A composite reading from 220 NOAA stations, 41 satellite swaths and 14 peer-reviewed indices, condensed into a single number — and the dozen vital signs underneath it.
↓ 4 pts vs. 2014 baseline · 6 of 10 subindices in decline

NOAA NCEI · 2024 Q4
        NASA PACE · v3.1
        Argo Float Array
        n = 3,924 stations

A score of 62 places the global ocean in the stable band — above the stressed threshold but below the long-term mean of 67 last recorded in 2014. The pull is downward: four of ten goals lost ground year-over-year.
Higher is healthier. The composite weights ten policy-relevant goals defined by Halpern et al. (2012) and updated annually by NOAA's Office of National Marine Sanctuaries.
Each category is itself a composite of 4–7 underlying indicators. Together they describe what the ocean is providing — and what is being asked of it.
SOURCE · NOAA NCEI · WORLDFISH · UNEP-WCMC · 2014–2024 ROLLING MEAN
Two co-trending stresses. As CO₂ dissolves into surface waters it lowers pH; as the ocean warms it holds less oxygen. Both curves cross critical thresholds for calcifying species after 2018.
of climate-system warming absorbed by oceans

A composite snapshot of a temperate-reef cross-section. Bleached coral silhouettes (coral / amber) front kelp-forest assemblages whose canopy biomass has declined 38% since 2000 in measured Pacific transects.
The 2024 methodology added two new sub-indicators — marine heatwave intensity and high-seas governance compliance. Three more, including soundscape integrity, enter the 2026 cycle.
An interactive infographic journal translating NOAA and peer-reviewed ocean science into dense, visually rich data stories.

The lens widens, slowly
          The 2024 methodology added two new sub-indicators — marine heatwave intensity and high-seas governance compliance. Three more, including soundscape integrity, enter t
The composite ocean health score has slipped from 66 to 62 across the last decade, a pace of −0.4 points per year. At current trajectory, the index reaches the "stressed" band by 2049.
Species-extinction risk and habitat-condition sub-metrics dropped 3 points in 2024 — the steepest single-year fall among any sub-dial in the last decade.

visibleText: Deep Current Vol. 07 Ocean Health Coral Systems Plastic Tides Temperature Rise Action Atlas Issue 07 · Scorecard 2024 The State of the Ocean. A composite reading from 220 NOAA stations, 41 satellite swaths and 14 peer-reviewed indices, condensed into a single number — and the dozen vital signs underneath it. 2024 Composite Reading 62 / 100 OHI ↓ 4 pts vs. 2014 baseline · 6 of 10 subindices in decline Reporting Period JAN — DEC 2024 Sources NOAA · NASA OCO · IPCC AR6 Published 14 MAR 2025 On this page Composite Index Six Sub-dials Vital Signs Chemistry & Oxygen Benthic Snapshot Depth Scale 0 m · EPI 200 m 1000 m · MESO 4000 m · ABYSS 11 km · HADAL Index Key 80–100 · Healthy 60–79 · Stable 40–59 · Stressed 20–39 · Degraded 0–19 · Critical Data Vintage NOAA NCEI · 2024 Q4 NASA PACE · v3.1 Argo Float Array n = 3,924 stations § 01 / Composite Ocean Health Index, 2024 FIG. 1 · GAUGE Composite reading across ten weighted goals Score / 100 · weighted by 2024 methodology Reading 62 /100 0 25 50 75 100 62 OHI · 2024 Reading interpretation Stable, but slipping. A score of 62 places the global ocean in the stable band — above the stressed threshold but below the long-term mean of 67 last recorded in 2014. The pull is downward: four of ten goals lost ground year-over-year. Best Goal 81 TOURISM & RECREATION Worst Goal 43 BIODIVERSITY Higher is healthier. The composite weights ten policy-relevant goals defined by Halpern et al. (2012) and updated annually by NOAA's Office of National Marine Sanctuaries. SOURCE · NOAA OHI 2024 · n=3,924 STATIONS −4 Net points lost since 2014. The composite ocean health score has slipped from 66 to 62 across the last decade, a pace of −0.4 points per year. At current trajectory, the index reaches the "stressed" band by 2049. § 02 / Sub-dials Six categories, six readings. FIG. 2 · DIAL SET Each category is itself a composite of 4–7 underlying indicators. Together they describe what the ocean is providing — and what is being asked of it. 43 Biodiversity −3 yr/yr 58 Clean Waters −1 yr/yr 67 Food Provision +2 yr/yr 54 Carbon Storage −2 yr/yr 70 Coastal Protection ±0 yr/yr 81 Tourism +4 yr/yr 43 Biodiversity is the lagging signal. Species-extinction risk and habitat-condition sub-metrics dropped 3 points in 2024 — the steepest single-year fall among any sub-dial in the last decade. § 03 / Vital Signs Twelve ocean metrics, 2014–2024. FIG. 3 · SPARKLINES Dissolved O₂ · ml/L 4.21 ▼ 0.18 vs 2014 Surface pH 7.92 ▼ 0.12 vs 2014 SST anomaly · °C +0.91 ▲ 0.42 vs 2014 Phytoplankton · mg/m³ 0.38 ▼ 6% vs 2014 Sea level rise · mm/yr 4.5 ▲ 1.2 vs 2014 Coral cover · % 28 ▼ 9 pts vs 2014 Fish stocks sustainable · % 62.3 ▲ 1.4 vs 2014 Plastic load · MT 11.0 ▲ 38% vs 2014 MPA coverage · % 8.2 ▲ 3.1 vs 2014 Marine heatwave days 142 ▲ 71% vs 2014 Seagrass extent · 1000 km² 163 ▼ 8% vs 2014 Mangrove gain · ha/yr +19k ▲ since 2018 SOURCE · NOAA NCEI · WORLDFISH · UNEP-WCMC · 2014–2024 ROLLING MEAN § 04 / Chemistry The acidifying, deoxygenating ocean. FIG. 4 · DUAL AXIS Surface pH against dissolved oxygen, 1990–2024 Global mean · 0–100 m mixed layer pH 8.04 → 7.92 O₂ 4.39 → 4.21 8.10 8.00 7.92 7.85 4.5 4.3 4.1 3.9 1990 2000 2010 2020 2024 2020 · pH 7.94 SURFACE pH DISSOLVED O₂ Two co-trending stresses. As CO₂ dissolves into surface waters it lowers pH; as the ocean warms it holds less oxygen. Both curves cross critical thresholds for calcifying species after 2018. Acidification −0.12 pH ≈ 30% more H⁺ ions vs. pre-industrial Deoxygenation −2% /decade 700+ documented hypoxic dead zones Heat Uptake 90% excess of climate-system warming absorbed by oceans "We are not running out of ocean. We are running out of functional ocean — the ocean that fixes carbon, feeds protein and buffers our coastlines." — Dr. Maren Voss, IOPAN · cited in NOAA NCEI 2024 Outlook § 05 / Benthic Snapshot What the dial does not show. FIG. 5 · CROSS-SECTION 0 m 50 m 150 m SEABED A composite snapshot of a temperate-reef cross-section. Bleached coral silhouettes (coral / amber) front kelp-forest assemblages whose canopy biomass has declined 38% since 2000 in measured Pacific transects. What the index undercounts Sub-200 m benthic ecosystems Cumulative chemical stressors (PFAS, PCBs) Underwater noise pollution Genetic-level biodiversity loss The lens widens, slowly The 2024 methodology added two new sub-indicators — marine heatwave intensity and high-seas governance compliance. Three more, including soundscape integrity, enter the 2026 cycle. Region OHI 2024 Δ 10-yr Leading Pressure North Atlantic 66 −3 Warming, fisheries Tropical Pacific 58 −6 Coral bleaching Indian Ocean 54 −5 Plastic load, heat Southern Ocean 71 +1 Krill harvest pressure Arctic 59 −7 Ice loss, acidification Mediterranean 48 −4 Eutrophication, tourism Continue → Coral Systems Skip to Action Atlas Deep Current An interactive infographic journal translating NOAA and peer-reviewed ocean science into dense, visually rich data stories.
